paragraphs.text: deliveroo
This position paper outlines the position of Deliveroo as regards the proposals outlined within the European Commission's public consultation on the Digital Fairness Act (DFA).

Deliveroo stands behind the ambition of the European Commission to protect consumers within the Union, building a fair and trusted economy. However, at a time when there is a renewed focus on both simplification and the overall competitiveness of European businesses on the global stage, the introduction of additional legislation must be duly weighed against its stated aim and the extent to which existing policies and frameworks are sufficient. We strongly urge the Commission to reconsider and evaluate the extent to which many of the practices detailed in the public consultation are already legislated for, as well as the overall scope of the Digital Fairness Act

When referring to 'dark patterns' it is important to clearly distinguish between genuine malicious activity and practices that are both commonplace and intended to enhance usability. While some of the activities presented by the Commission within this section of the public consultation (e.g. confirm shaming) should not be employed by any actors operating within the Union, many others are defined too loosely and in such a way that, if introduced without further precision, could have wide-ranging and unintended consequences. This lack of precision - and the uncertainty it creates - will stifle innovation within the European Union.

section_path: Dark patterns and the risks of oversimplification

evidence_quotes.quote: Unpacking this, when does suggesting an item based on a pattern of historical choices become 'presenting choices in a leading manner'?

paragraphs.text: Unpacking this, when does suggesting an item based on a pattern of historical choices become 'presenting choices in a leading manner'? Platforms should not be considered as bad faith actors for merely trying to improve customer experiences.

section_path: Dark patterns and the risks of oversimplification

evidence_quotes.quote: How will it be distinguished between what constitutes 'nagging' a consumer vs merely prompting them regarding a product that they have already expressed an interest in, and potentially placed in an online basket?

paragraphs.text: How will it be distinguished between what constitutes 'nagging' a consumer vs merely prompting them regarding a product that they have already expressed an interest in, and potentially placed in an online basket? Here again, a clear distinction must be made in order to ensure that only the worst excesses are punished so as to enable the vast majority of businesses to continue as normal.

section_path: Dark patterns and the risks of oversimplification

evidence_quotes.quote: variable fees calculated based on the value of a basket

paragraphs.text: Do variable fees calculated based on the value of a basket count as those being snuck into consumers' online baskets if there is no flat fee, and where there is clear information about the existence of these fees upfront? Any new rules in this space would have to take due consideration of the fact that some fees cannot be 100% accurately communicated to consumers until they have finalised their orders.

section_path: Dark patterns and the risks of oversimplification

paragraphs.text: Without due consideration for such questions, there is a genuine risk that the introduction of additional requirements would have considerable negative impacts on countless European businesses. Deliveroo therefore urges the Commission to consider all potential implications, intended and otherwise, of the options presented in the public consultation before proceeding with measures intended to tackle so-called dark patterns.

section_path: Dark patterns and the risks of oversimplification

evidence_quotes.quote: personalisation should be a help rather than a hindrance
tackle personalised advertising that targets minors or vulnerable individuals

paragraphs.text: At its core, personalisation should be a help rather than a hindrance, ensuring consumers are shown products, services or content that is relevant and interesting to them, and we welcome the acknowledgement by the Commission, within the consultation, of the benefits that personalisation brings. Deliveroo clearly and unequivocally supports any action to tackle personalised advertising that targets minors or vulnerable individuals, and only collects data required for age verification purposes. It should be noted that the online protection of minors, including elements pertaining to advertising, is already expressly addressed under Article 28 of the Digital Services Act.

evidence_quotes.quote: An overly prohibitive approach to personalisation, and the data that apps are able to use, risks negatively affecting the relevance of content and usability of a wide variety of websites and applications that use this data that is freely shared by consumers.
Local delivery platforms, such as Deliveroo, may suggest nearby restaurants that a consumer has previously ordered from and enjoyed ahead of others.

paragraphs.text: An overly prohibitive approach to personalisation, and the data that apps are able to use, risks negatively affecting the relevance of content and usability of a wide variety of websites and applications that use this data that is freely shared by consumers. Local delivery platforms, such as Deliveroo, may suggest nearby restaurants that a consumer has previously ordered from and enjoyed ahead of others. Any such information related to their enjoyment, or otherwise, of an item is shared freely, and we therefore logically feel they may wish to order from that same restaurant again. This personalisation is not an attempt to misguide or deceive, rather intended to facilitate a quicker and simpler user experience.

evidence_quotes.quote: opting in/out of receiving more personalised adverts and content when online

paragraphs.text: Furthermore, due consideration needs to be given to the existing rights of citizens when it comes to opting in/out of receiving more personalised adverts and content when online and the regulations already in place to try and provide as much flexibility to consumers as possible, such as the DSA and GDPR. We urge the Commission to refrain from introducing new and potentially duplicative requirements that would risk both negatively impacting consumers' experiences online and burdening companies. Any newly introduced policy measures should be accompanied by thorough impact assessments and, where relevant and necessary, addressed through amendments to existing legislation.

Businesses such as Deliveroo, understand the negative impact on sales that additional, unforeseen costs added at later stages in a transaction have on completion rates and take steps to mitigate these risks insofar as is possible. Fees added at checkout are calculated based on factors including the value of goods/services purchased and it is not possible to do more than approximate these costs beforehand. Details of fixed fees are shown to customers up front (e.g. delivery fees) while others (e.g. service fees, and small order fees applied to orders below a certain threshold), are detailed within our app but can only be calculated/applied at checkout. Without further explanation of what would constitute presenting these mandatory costs and fees upfront, any associated costs could not unreasonably be considered to be an act of drip pricing under the current terminology in the public consultation.

section_path: "Drip pricing" or delivery: where to draw the line?

evidence_quotes.quote: the transparency of pricing for consumers

paragraphs.text: We therefore call on the Commission to reflect further on how any proposals aimed at improving the transparency of pricing for consumers would work and be applied practically.

section_path: "Drip pricing" or delivery: where to draw the line?

evidence_quotes.quote: Such subscriptions are in and of themselves flexible in terms of the length of contract, payment frequency, and the provision of services included, with consumers given the choice to decide which best fits their needs or budget.

paragraphs.text: Subscription-based services have grown in popularity in recent years, from streaming services to delivery platforms such as Deliveroo. Such subscriptions are in and of themselves flexible in terms of the length of contract, payment frequency, and the provision of services included, with consumers given the choice to decide which best fits their needs or budget. It is commonplace for longer-term contracts to be offered at preferential rates, in some cases charged on an annual rather than a monthly basis. Such offers are intended to save the consumer money, and often include additional benefits that lead to an enhanced customer experience (e.g. lower delivery fees, cashback). At Deliveroo we offer our customers a quick and simple option for ending their subscriptions, and they are always able to speak with a person at any point in relation to their contract with us.

evidence_quotes.quote: consumers being able to terminate automatically extended subscriptions at any time with short notice is simply not practical.
any reimbursement so as not to leave the system open to major abuse and massively disadvantage businesses.

paragraphs.text: A common sense approach is needed as regards any policy actions aimed at tackling perceived unfair practices in the area of subscriptions. The proposal for consumers being able to terminate automatically extended subscriptions at any time with short notice is simply not practical. Many subscriptions include benefits that would otherwise be paid-for services by non-subscription users which would need to be factored into any reimbursement so as not to leave the system open to major abuse and massively disadvantage businesses.

Policymakers should ensure that any new requirements brought in to tackle perceived negative practices relating to subscriptions are simple, practical and strike the right balance between the need for increased consumer protection and business continuity.

The opportunity to provide input concerning the areas in which measures can be simplified is a welcome one. However, even in this instance, the broader simplification agenda is used as an avenue for further EU action, under the auspices of reducing single market fragmentation.

Furthermore, the consultation effectively acknowledges the overlap between existing policies including the Audiovisual Media Services Directive, the Digital Services Act, the Digital Markets Act, the Artificial Intelligence Act, or the EU Digital Identity Framework as regards consumer protection. This concession should serve as evidence of the concerns of businesses operating across the Union when it comes to further regulation that, far from simplifying things, will only make compliance more challenging.

Deliveroo's business model is fundamentally consumer-centric, seeking to transform the way people shop and eat, bringing the neighbourhood to our customers' doors by connecting consumers, restaurants, shops and riders. Our consumer value proposition (CVP) is central to our direction and decision-making, knowing that we operate in a highly competitive and congested market. As such, we take steps to ensure that we are a good faith actor, especially in areas relating to consumer protection, and we do not consider ourselves to be intentionally in breach of any of the practices outlined in the consultation. However, several of the terms and definitions used in the public consultation are defined so loosely as to raise major concerns over the potential direction and impact of the Digital Fairness Act on European businesses. It is therefore essential for the Commission to consider the following:
Ensuring that any new policy proposals are accompanied with full and detailed impact assessments, coming forward with new requirements only when benefits for consumers would be markedly improved;
Further refining the vast majority of proposals presented in the consultation process, to avoid further challenges for European companies as regards their obligations;

Avoid legislating on practices outlined in the public consultation that are already covered, at least in part, in existing pieces of legislation, including the UCPD, GDPR and DSA. Further consideration should be given as to whether guidance on the correct application of existing requirements would suffice in many of the areas covered before additional requirements are explored.
Notwithstanding these concerns, we stand ready to work collaboratively with the European Commission to improve both the overall business environment and consumer protection level in the EU.

Deliveroo is changing the way people eat and shop by bringing the neighbourhood to your door, unlocking a wealth of hyperlocal choice across restaurants, grocery and retail, with fast and reliable delivery, at the right price.

Founded in 2013 by William Shu and Greg Orlowski, Deliveroo joined forces with DoorDash (NASDAQ: DASH) in 2025. Together, we operate in over 40 countries, nine of which are with Deliveroo's product and brand, with four in the EU (France, Italy, Belgium and Ireland). You can read more on the Deliveroo website.
The contribution of the Central European Lawyers Initiative (CELI) is provided in the attached document.
